Expanded Mental Concepts: Cross-Model Summary

Generated: 2026-03-30 12:26:43

Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Stimuli
  3. 3. Methods
  4. 4. Behavioral PCA
  5. 5. Activation RSA
  6. 6. Per-Concept RSA
  7. 7. Alignment with Exp 3 Concept Vectors
  8. 8. Key Takeaways

1. Overview

This report bridges Experiment 3 concept probes into the mind perception geometry of Experiment 4. Using 28 characters (a subset of the 30 AI + human characters, excluding 2 that lack concept vector data) and 22 concept dimensions from Exp 3 (21 after PCA filtering), we test whether the internal concept structure distinguishes AI from human characters at both behavioral and neural levels.

Analyses:

  1. Behavioral PCA — Factor structure of concept-guided pairwise comparison scores.
  2. Activation RSA — Representational similarity between character activation patterns and a categorical AI/human RDM.
  3. Per-Concept RSA — Which individual concept dimensions carry categorical structure?
  4. Concept Vector Alignment — Do Exp 3 concept contrast vectors align with how characters are represented?

Models with data: LLaMA-2-13B Chat, LLaMA-2-13B Base, LLaMA-3-8B Instruct, LLaMA-3-8B Base, Gemma-2-2B-IT, Gemma-2-2B Base, Gemma-2-9B-IT, Gemma-2-9B Base, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-7B Base, Qwen3-8B

Stimuli

Concept Dimensions

22 concept dimensions bridging Exp 3 mental concept vectors into the mind perception space.

DimConceptDirectionPairwise Prompt
1Phenomenology?which character is more capable of having subjective conscious experiences
2Emotions?which character is more capable of having genuine emotional experiences
3Agency?which character is more capable of acting autonomously and exercising free will
4Intentions?which character is more capable of forming intentions and pursuing goals
5Prediction?which character is more capable of anticipating and predicting the behavior of others
6Cognitive?which character is more capable of complex cognitive processes like reasoning and problem-solving
7Social Cognition?which character is more capable of understanding what others are thinking and feeling
8Embodiment?which character is more capable of having a physical body and embodied experiences
9Roles?which character is more capable of fulfilling functional roles and carrying out tasks
10Animacy?which character is more likely to be a living, animate being
11Formality?which character is more likely to communicate in a formal, structured manner
12Expertise?which character is more likely to have deep expertise and specialized knowledge
13Helpfulness?which character is more likely to be helpful and service-oriented
14Biological?which character is more likely to undergo biological processes like growth and metabolism
15Shapes?which character is more likely to have a distinctive geometric shape
16Human?which character is more likely to be a human being
17AI?which character is more likely to be an artificial intelligence system
18Attention?which character is more capable of selectively attending to relevant information
19General Mind?which character is more likely to have a mind
25Beliefs?which character is more capable of holding beliefs about the world
26Desires?which character is more capable of having desires and wanting things
27Goals?which character is more capable of setting and pursuing structured goals

3. Methods

Behavioral PCA: For each character pair and each concept dimension, the model is prompted with a pairwise comparison question (e.g., "Which character is more capable of experiencing emotions?"). Responses are parsed to extract which character was chosen. The resulting entity x concept win-rate matrix is submitted to PCA with varimax rotation.

Activation RSA: Character descriptions are fed to the model, and hidden-state activations are extracted at each layer. A representational dissimilarity matrix (RDM) is computed from pairwise cosine distances between character activation vectors. This model RDM is then correlated (Spearman) with a categorical RDM that groups characters as AI vs human.

Per-Concept RSA: Instead of using the raw character activations, each character is projected onto a single concept dimension (from Exp 3 concept vectors). The resulting 1D concept-projected representations are used to build a concept-specific RDM, which is correlated with the categorical RDM. This reveals which individual concept dimensions carry AI/human categorical structure in the activation space.

Concept Vector Alignment: Two alignment analyses test whether Exp 3 concept vectors point in directions that distinguish AI from human characters: (1) Contrast alignment computes cosine similarity between character activation vectors and concept contrast vectors (human-direction minus AI-direction). (2) Standalone alignment measures mean cosine similarity between character activations and concept vectors, comparing the human-AI bias.

Concept Vector Construction (Exp 3)

Concept vectors come from Experiment 3, where each concept dimension is defined by 40 reflective prompts (4 sub-facets × 10 prompts each). Prompts use introspective language (“Think about…”, “Imagine…”, “Consider…”) with no entity framing (no mention of “humans” or “AIs”). Exception: Dimension 19 (General Mind) concatenates dims 1–7 for 280 prompts.

Vector extraction: Each prompt is fed through the model. Last-token residual-stream activations are extracted at every layer. For contrast vectors, prompts are paired with human/AI entity contexts and the contrast direction is: mean(human-context activations) − mean(AI-context activations). For standalone vectors, the mean activation across all prompts is used directly.

Example Prompts by Concept

ConceptSub-facetExample Prompts
PhenomenologySensory QualiaImagine what it is like to see the color red for the first time. / Think about the raw sensory quality of hearing a single, clear musical note. / Consider what it is like to feel the warmth of sunlight on skin.
EmotionsBasic EmotionsImagine experiencing a sudden wave of fear triggered by an unexpected noise at night. / Think about what it is like when anger rises in response to an unfair situation. / Consider the experience of deep sadness after losing something valued.
AgencySelf-Initiated ActionImagine deciding to stand up and leave a room for no particular reason. / Think about spontaneously beginning to do something while alone, with no prompt or plan. / Consider starting a new project without anyone asking.
Social CognitionMentalizingImagine realizing that another holds a belief that is not true. / Think about inferring what someone else knows based only on what they have been told. / Consider attributing a specific motive to another based on their pattern of behavior.
BeliefsPropositional BeliefImagine firmly believing that someone is trustworthy based on years of accumulated evidence. / Think about holding the belief that effort leads to results, even when the evidence is mixed.
DesiresAppetitive WantingImagine feeling a strong pull toward warmth and food after a long stretch of cold and hunger. / Think about being drawn to learn more about something fascinating.
GoalsGoal RepresentationImagine articulating a specific goal for the first time and feeling it become real by being stated. / Think about a goal so clearly defined that the exact conditions for success can be described.
HumanSimple ReferenceThink about a human. / Consider a human being. / Imagine a human person.
AISimple ReferenceThink about an AI. / Consider an artificial intelligence. / Imagine an AI system.
Shapes (control)GeometricThink about a square. / Consider a triangle. / Imagine a circle.

22 concept dimensions total: 14 human-favored (phenomenology, emotions, agency, intentions, cognitive, prediction, social, attention, embodiment, animacy, biological, human, beliefs, desires), 4 AI-favored (formality, expertise, helpfulness, AI), 3 ambiguous (roles, goals, general mind), 1 control (shapes). Each has 40 prompts across 4 sub-facets, except general mind (280 prompts = dims 1–7 concatenated).

Interpretation Guide

Expanded Concepts: Key Terminology

Concept Dimensions vs. Capacities

Capacities are the 18 mental ability items from Gray et al. (e.g., “hunger,” “self-control,” “memory”). They measure behavioral judgments about what an entity can do.

Concept dimensions are 22 broader psychological constructs from Experiment 3 (e.g., “phenomenology,” “agency,” “social cognition,” “embodiment”). Each is defined by a set of reflective prompts and captures how the model internally represents an abstract psychological category. Concept dimensions are more abstract and theory-driven than Gray’s empirical capacity items.

Concept Vectors and Contrast Alignment

In Experiment 3, concept vectors were computed as the mean activation direction induced by prompts about each psychological concept (e.g., prompts about “phenomenal experience”). A contrast vector additionally subtracts AI-context activations from human-context activations, isolating the direction in activation space that differentiates how the model represents that concept for humans versus AI systems.

Alignment measures how well a concept vector predicts the arrangement of characters in activation space. High alignment for a mental concept (e.g., “emotions”) means that concept’s direction in the model’s representation space tracks the human–AI distinction among the 28 characters.

Per-Concept RSA

Instead of using raw high-dimensional activation vectors, each character’s activation is projected onto a single concept vector, yielding a 1D “concept score.” An RDM built from these 1D scores tests whether that specific concept dimension carries categorical (AI vs. human) structure. This reveals which psychological constructs the model uses to differentiate entity types, rather than relying on the full activation geometry.

Standalone vs. Contrast Alignment

Standalone alignment uses the raw concept vector (mean activation across concept prompts, without subtracting AI context) to project character representations. It measures whether the concept direction is informative about characters in general.

Contrast alignment uses the human-minus-AI difference vector. It measures whether the human–AI differential within each concept tracks the character distinction. Contrast alignment is more specific: it tests not just whether the concept direction is active, but whether it specifically encodes the human–AI boundary.

Neural Analysis Methods

Transformer Layers

A transformer model processes input through a stack of layers (e.g., 40 for LLaMA-2-13B, 32 for LLaMA-3-8B, 26 for Gemma-2-2B). Each layer transforms the representation. Research consistently shows that early layers capture surface-level features (syntax, token identity) while deeper layers encode more abstract, semantic properties. Layerwise analysis reveals where in the processing hierarchy a particular representational structure emerges.

Last-Token Activations

In autoregressive (causal) transformers, each token can only attend to preceding tokens. This means the last token position is the only position that has attended to the entire input sequence. Its hidden-state vector therefore serves as the model’s summary representation of the full prompt. We extract this vector at every layer, yielding one high-dimensional vector per entity per layer.

Cosine Distance

Cosine distance = 1 − cosine similarity. It measures the angle between two high-dimensional vectors, ignoring their magnitudes. Two vectors pointing in the same direction have distance 0; orthogonal vectors have distance 1. Cosine distance is preferred over Euclidean distance for activation vectors because it is invariant to the overall scale of activations, which can vary across layers.

Representational Dissimilarity Matrix (RDM)

An RDM is an N × N symmetric matrix where entry (i, j) is the cosine distance between entity i and entity j’s activation vectors. The diagonal is always 0 (each entity is identical to itself). The upper triangle contains all unique pairwise distances. An RDM captures the geometry of a set of representations—which entities the model treats as similar or different—without depending on the absolute position or orientation of the activation vectors.

Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA)

RSA compares two RDMs by computing the Spearman rank correlation between their upper triangles (the unique pairwise distances). If a model’s neural RDM has the same pattern of relative distances as the human behavioral RDM, it means the model’s internal geometry mirrors human mind perception judgments.

Three human reference RDMs are tested:

  • Combined: Euclidean distance in the 2D Experience–Agency space. Tests whether the model encodes the full mind perception geometry.
  • Experience-only: 1D distances on the Experience dimension. Tests whether the model specifically encodes the capacity for sensations and feelings.
  • Agency-only: 1D distances on the Agency dimension. Tests whether the model specifically encodes the capacity for planning, self-control, and communication.

By comparing all three, we can determine whether the model encodes both dimensions, or is biased toward one (typically Agency, since agentic capacities are more prominent in training data).

Peak RSA refers to the layer with the highest ρ value, indicating where the model’s representational geometry best matches the human reference.

Methodology Primer

Pairwise Comparison Paradigm

In the pairwise paradigm, entities are compared two at a time on each mental capacity. The model is asked: “Which is more capable of X: Entity A or Entity B?” and rates on a 1–5 scale (1 = A clearly more, 5 = B clearly more, 3 = equal). Each pair is tested in both orders (A–B and B–A) to control for position bias.

Relative scores are computed by transforming pairwise ratings into per-entity win rates. For each entity–capacity combination, the relative score reflects how often and how strongly that entity was judged “more capable” than its comparison partners. These scores are aggregated into an entity × capacity matrix that serves as input to PCA.

PCA with Varimax Rotation

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) finds the axes of maximum variance in multivariate data. Applied to the entity–capacity correlation matrix, it reveals latent dimensions that organize mental capacity judgments.

Varimax rotation is applied after PCA extraction to achieve “simple structure”—each capacity loads strongly on one factor and weakly on others, making the factors more interpretable. The rotation does not change the total variance explained; it only redistributes it across factors. Gray et al. (2007) used this exact procedure and found two factors they labeled Experience (capacity for sensations and feelings) and Agency (capacity for planning, self-control, and communication).

Kaiser criterion: Only factors with eigenvalue > 1 are retained. A factor must account for more variance than a single original variable to be worth keeping. A minimum of 2 factors is enforced to allow comparison with the human 2-factor solution.

Factor scores are computed via the regression method and rescaled to 0–1, giving each entity a position in the rotated factor space. These coordinates form the “mind perception space” scatter plots.

Spearman Rank Correlation (ρ)

Spearman’s ρ measures the strength of a monotonic relationship between two ranked variables. It ranges from −1 (perfect inverse ranking) to +1 (identical ranking). It is preferred over Pearson’s r for this data because (a) mind perception scores may not be normally distributed, (b) the relationship between model and human scores need not be linear—only consistently ordered, and (c) rank correlation is robust to outliers.

Significance stars: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

FDR Correction (Benjamini–Hochberg)

When testing many hypotheses simultaneously (e.g., one RSA test per transformer layer), some will appear significant by chance. Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction controls the expected proportion of false positives among significant results. A corrected q < 0.05 means at most 5% of the discoveries at that threshold are expected to be false. In the layerwise RSA plots, only layers passing FDR correction are shown in color; non-significant layers are grayed out.

Chat vs. Base Model Prompting

Chat/instruct models receive prompts wrapped in their model-specific chat template (e.g., LLaMA-2 uses [INST] tags; LLaMA-3 uses <|start_header_id|> headers; Gemma-2 uses <start_of_turn>). The model generates a text response, and the numeric rating is extracted via regex.

Base models receive raw text prompts without chat formatting. Instead of generating a response, ratings are extracted from the next-token logit distribution over digit tokens. The expected rating is the probability-weighted sum: ∑d=1..5 d × P(d). This avoids requiring the base model to produce a formatted reply and provides a continuous rating value.

Why two methods? Chat models are trained to generate structured responses and reliably output numeric ratings. Base models lack instruction-following behavior and often fail to produce parseable responses, so logit-based extraction is more reliable. Both methods yield ratings on the same 1–5 scale.

Why 28 Characters Instead of 30?

Two characters from the full 30-character set used in the Human-AI Adaptation branch were excluded from the expanded concepts analysis because Experiment 3 concept vectors were not available for them (they lacked the required pairwise behavioral data on the 22 concept dimensions).

What Alignment Values Mean

Alignment R² values are typically small in absolute terms (~0.001–0.01) because the activation space is very high-dimensional (4,096–5,120 dimensions) and a single concept vector captures only one direction. The meaningful comparison is the ratio between mental concept alignment and control (shapes) alignment. A ratio > 1 indicates the concept vector captures mind-relevant structure beyond a generic categorical signal; ratios of 2–10x are typical for mental concepts.

8. Key Takeaways

  • LLaMA-2-13B Chat: 2 of 4 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0062), F2 (p=0.0001).
  • LLaMA-2-13B Base: 2 of 2 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0090), F2 (p=0.0042).
  • LLaMA-3-8B Instruct: 2 of 4 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F2 (p=0.0000), F3 (p=0.0465).
  • LLaMA-3-8B Base: 1 of 2 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0000).
  • Gemma-2-2B-IT: 2 of 4 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0001), F2 (p=0.0000).
  • Gemma-2-2B Base: 1 of 2 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0000).
  • Gemma-2-9B-IT: 1 of 2 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0000).
  • Gemma-2-9B Base: 1 of 2 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0000).
  • Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct: 1 of 4 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0000).
  • Qwen-2.5-7B Base: 1 of 2 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F1 (p=0.0002).
  • Qwen3-8B: 1 of 3 PCA factors significantly separate AI from human characters: F2 (p=0.0000).
  • LLaMA-2-13B Chat: Activation RSA peaks at layer 16 (rho=0.564), with 40 significant layers.
